answer:
There are several Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers available for WhatsApp, primarily focused on the official WhatsApp Business Cloud API [1][2][3][4]. These servers allow AI assistants like Claude or Cursor to perform actions such as sending messages (text, media, templates), managing contacts, and handling business profiles [1][5][6][4][7].

Most implementations provide tools for both local development (using stdio transport) and remote deployment (using HTTP/SSE transport) [1][5][3][4].

Key considerations include:
- Official vs. Unofficial: Most repositories (e.g., tkhattar14, FredShred7, Gonzalez8) exclusively use the official Meta Cloud API to ensure compliance with WhatsApp Terms of Service and prevent account bans [1][2].
- Capabilities: Feature sets vary by repository, ranging from basic messaging and template management to advanced functionalities like AI auto-replies, anti-spam protection, WhatsApp Flows, and multi-tenancy [1][3][4][7].
- Configuration: You typically configure these by adding the server to your MCP client’s (e.g., Claude Desktop) configuration file and providing necessary environment variables like META_ACCESS_TOKEN and META_PHONE_NUMBER_ID [1][5][6].

For specific setups, consult the documentation of the repository you choose, as configuration requirements (such as port numbers and authentication tokens) depend on the specific implementation chosen [1][5][3][8].
